files.complete_text:
LESSON II.

Work and Business.

SECOND WEEK IN FEBRUARY.

THE IDEAL WOMAN.

PROVERBS 31:10-31.

References: Prov. 31:10-31; Eph. 5:22-33; Col. 3:18-25; I Tim. 2:9-15; Titus 2:3-5; I Pet. 3:1-7.

The thirty-first chapter of Proverbs gives us a picture of the ideal woman. It is a picture drawn by an inspired pen, and one which has been the standard of womanly excellence for thousands of years. Let us study it carefully, and see what lessons we may learn from it.

The chapter opens with the words of King Lemuel, which his mother taught him. She warns him against wine and strong drink, and against giving his strength unto women. Then she tells him what manner of woman he should seek for a wife.

"Who can find a virtuous woman? for her price is far above rubies." This is the opening verse of the description of the ideal woman. The word "virtuous" here means much more than mere chastity. It means a woman of worth, of excellence, of noble character. Such a woman is rare and precious.

"The heart of her husband doth safely trust in her, so that he shall have no need of spoil." She is trustworthy. Her husband can depend upon her absolutely. He knows that she will be true to him and to their home interests.

"She will do him good and not evil all the days of her life." She is loyal and devoted. Her influence upon her husband is always for good.

"She seeketh wool, and flax, and worketh willingly with her hands." She is industrious. She does not shrink from work, but enters into it with a willing spirit.

"She is like the merchants' ships; she bringeth her food from afar." She is provident. She plans ahead and makes provision for her household.

"She riseth also while it is yet night, and giveth meat to her household, and a portion to her maidens." She is an early riser, and sees that her family is properly fed.

"She considereth a field, and buyeth it: with the fruit of her hands she planteth a vineyard." She has business ability. She can invest money wisely and make it productive.

"She girdeth her loins with strength, and strengtheneth her arms." She keeps herself physically fit for her work.

"She perceiveth that her merchandise is good: her candle goeth not out by night." She takes pride in the quality of her work, and is willing to work late when necessary.

"She layeth her hands to the spindle, and her hands hold the distaff." She is skilled in the domestic arts.

"She stretcheth out her hand to the poor; yea, she reacheth forth her hands to the needy." She is charitable and kind to those less fortunate.

"She is not afraid of the snow for her household: for all her household are clothed with scarlet." She provides well for her family.

"She maketh herself coverings of tapestry; her clothing is silk and purple." She dresses well and tastefully.

"Her husband is known in the gates, when he sitteth among the elders of the land." Her good management of the home enables her husband to take his place in public affairs.

"She maketh fine linen, and selleth it; and delivereth girdles unto the merchant." She has business enterprises of her own.

"Strength and honour are her clothing; and she shall rejoice in time to come." She has character and dignity.

"She openeth her mouth with wisdom; and in her tongue is the law of kindness." She is wise in her speech and kind in her manner.

"She looketh well to the ways of her household, and eateth not the bread of idleness." She is a good manager and is not lazy.

"Her children arise up, and call her blessed; her husband also, and he praiseth her." She is appreciated by her family.

"Many daughters have done virtuously, but thou excellest them all." She surpasses other good women.

"Favour is deceitful, and beauty is vain: but a woman that feareth the Lord, she shall be praised." True worth is not in outward appearance, but in character and godliness.

"Give her of the fruit of her hands; and let her own works praise her in the gates." She deserves recognition for her achievements.

This is indeed a wonderful picture of womanhood. Here is a woman who combines all the virtues—industry, thrift, business ability, charity, wisdom, kindness, and above all, the fear of the Lord. She is a helpmeet indeed to her husband, a wise mother to her children, and a blessing to all who know her.

QUESTIONS.

What is meant by a "virtuous woman"?
What are some of the qualities of the ideal woman as described in Proverbs?
How does she help her husband?
What is her attitude toward work?
How does she provide for her household?
What business ability does she show?
How does she treat the poor?
What is said about her speech?
How is she regarded by her family?
What is the source of her true worth?
